content.description:
# Type Workshop — Visual Type Designer

A view that lets you visually construct a type definition and its views simultaneously, with a live preview of what an instance would look like as you add fields and choose field-views.

See also: [Future Directions §1.5](item://c0c0c0c0-0042-0000-0000-000000000000)

---

## The Problem Today

Creating a new type requires manually authoring 3–4 coordinated JSON items:

1. **Type-definition** — name, extends chain, preferredView, description
2. **Readonly view** — `for_type`, `ui_hints` mapping field paths to field-views, boilerplate code that calls `generic-view.render()`
3. **Editable view** — same structure but with `mode: "editable"` and editable field-views
4. **Set preferredView** back on the type-def pointing to the readonly view

You need to know: GUID conventions, the dot-path field syntax (`content.description`), which of the ~16 field-views exist, what options each one accepts (`level` for headings, `language` for code, `format` for timestamps, etc.), and how `generic-view` consumes `ui_hints`. None of this is discoverable from within Hobson itself.

**Principle gap:** This is the single biggest gap between Hobson's philosophy and its reality. The system aspires to be self-revealing, but type creation is opaque to anyone who doesn't already understand the internals.

---

## Design

### Layout: Two Panels

**Left — Type Designer:**

- **Name** — text input
- **Extends** — dropdown populated by querying all type-definition instances via `related-items-lib.getInstancesOf(TYPE_DEFINITION)`
- **Description** — markdown editor
- **Fields list** — the core of the tool. Each field row has:
  - **Field path** (e.g. `content.status`) — text input with autocomplete for common patterns (`name`, `content.*`)
  - **Label** — display name
  - **Field-view** — dropdown populated by querying all instances of the field-view type (`cccccccc-0000-0000-0000-000000000000`)
  - **Mode** — readonly / editable / both (generates the right hint per view)
  - **Options** — field-view-specific settings (heading level, code language, timestamp format, collapsible, dividerAfter, etc.)
  - Drag to reorder, button to remove

**Right — Live Preview:**

- Renders a mock instance through `generic-view` using the `ui_hints` being constructed in real-time
- Shows both readonly and editable previews in tabs

**Actions:**

- **Create Type** — generates the type-def + readonly view + editable view + sets preferredView, all via `api.set()`
- **Create Sample Instance** — optionally creates a blank item of the new type so you can see it immediately

---

## Design Decisions

### 1. Field-View Self-Description

The biggest gap. Field-views currently export `render(value, options, api)` but don't advertise what `options` they accept. The Type Workshop needs to know that `field-view-heading` accepts `level: 1-6`, that `field-view-timestamp` accepts `format: "full"|"date"|"time"|"relative"`, etc.

Options:

- **(a) Hardcode** — a lookup table in the Type Workshop mapping field-view names to their option schemas. Quick to build, breaks when new field-views are added.
- **(b) Field-view metadata convention** — field-views export an `optionsSchema` alongside `render`. The Type Workshop reads it dynamically. More work upfront, more aligned with Hobson's philosophy.
- **(c) Hybrid** (recommended) — ship with hardcoded defaults, but prefer `optionsSchema` when a field-view exports one. Build incrementally.

### 2. Editing Existing Types

Creating new types is the simpler case. Editing an existing type means:

- Finding which views have `content.for_type` pointing at this type (already possible via `related-items-lib.getViewsFor(typeId)`)
- Parsing their `ui_hints` back into the field designer
- Updating rather than creating on save

Valuable but could be a phase 2.

### 3. Live Preview Mechanics

The preview needs to render through `generic-view` with the in-progress `ui_hints`. Two approaches:

- **(a) Virtual item** (recommended) — construct a temporary item object in memory (never persisted) and pass it to `generic-view.render()` with a synthetic view context. Requires `generic-view` to accept an explicit `ui_hints` parameter rather than always fetching from `api.getViewId()`.
- **(b) Scratch item** — actually persist a draft item and view, re-render on each change, clean up on cancel. Simpler to implement since it uses the existing pipeline, but pollutes the item store with drafts.

Option (a) is cleaner but needs a small change to `generic-view` — a one-parameter addition to accept passed-in `ui_hints`.

### 4. View Generation: One View or Two?

The current convention is separate readonly and editable views. Options:

- Always generate both (simpler UX, matches convention)
- Let the user toggle per-field, generate a single mixed-mode view
- Generate both, let the user pick which fields appear in each

Recommendation: always generate a matched pair. It matches the existing convention and the user can customize later.

---

## Implementation

### Items to Create

1. **`type-workshop`** — the view item (type `aaaaaaaa-0000-0000-0000-000000000000`) containing the full UI
2. **`type-workshop-lib`** — optional library extracting the generation logic (GUID creation, `ui_hints` assembly, `api.set()` calls) so it's testable from the REPL
3. A widget-type instance or app-page to open it from

### Dependencies

- `generic-view` — for preview rendering (needs minor extension to accept explicit `ui_hints`)
- `related-items-lib` — for discovering types, field-views, and existing views
- `field-view-*` items — as the palette of available renderers

### Phases

**Phase 1 — Create-only, hardcoded field-view options:**
- Type designer panel with all fields
- Hardcoded option schemas for existing field-views
- Generates type-def + two views + sets preferredView
- No live preview yet (just a summary of what will be created)

**Phase 2 — Live preview:**
- Extend `generic-view` to accept explicit `ui_hints`
- Preview panel rendering a virtual instance in real-time

**Phase 3 — Edit existing types:**
- Load an existing type-def and its views into the designer
- Detect and parse existing `ui_hints` back into field rows
- Update-in-place on save

**Phase 4 — Dynamic field-view metadata:**
- Define `optionsSchema` convention for field-views
- Migrate existing field-views to export schemas
- Type Workshop reads schemas dynamically, falls back to hardcoded

---

## What It Exercises

Beyond the "self-revealing" principle:

- **Compositional rendering** — can `generic-view` be used inside another view's output?
- **Field-view discoverability** — do field-views need richer metadata?
- **Type/view indirection** — is the type → view → field-view layering too indirect, or does the indirection pay off when you have a tool that navigates it for you?
- **Self-reference** — the Type Workshop is a view item that creates other view items. Hobson building Hobson.
